title: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema) in Children
content:
Atopic Dermatitis in Children

Atopic dermatitis (AD), commonly known as eczema, is a chronic inflammatory skin condition affecting 10-20% of children. It's characterized by intense pruritus, xerosis, and characteristic inflammation patterns varying by age.

Pathophysiology

AD results from impaired skin barrier function due to reduced ceramide levels and filaggrin mutations, combined with Th2-skewed immune response and elevated IgE levels.

Clinical Presentation by Age

In infants, eczema appears on face, scalp, and extensor surfaces. In older children, it shifts to flexural surfaces including antecubital and popliteal fossae. Adolescents may present with facial or hand dermatitis.

Diagnostic Criteria

Diagnosis is clinical, based on Hanifin and Rajka or UK Working Party criteria. Key features include onset before age 2, pruritus, chronic relapsing course, xerosis, and characteristic morphology.

Management Strategies

First-line treatment focuses on emollients and skin care. Topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors treat mild to moderate disease. Severe cases may require systemic corticosteroids, phototherapy, or dupilumab.

Trigger Management

Common triggers include harsh soaps, fragrances, dust mites, animal dander, and stress. Bathing in lukewarm water with mild cleansers, soft breathable clothing, and humidity maintenance help prevent flares.

Prognosis

Many children experience improvement or remission by adolescence. Complications include secondary bacterial infection and increased risk of other atopic conditions.
meta_description: Atopic dermatitis in children: pathophysiology, clinical features, diagnosis, and evidence-based management strategies.

title: Cradle Cap: Infantile Seborrheic Dermatitis
content:
Cradle Cap: Infantile Seborrheic Dermatitis

Cradle cap is a benign inflammatory condition in newborns and infants (2 weeks to 12 months) presenting as greasy, yellowish or brownish scaling on the scalp and sebaceous areas. Unlike atopic dermatitis, it is non-pruritic and causes minimal discomfort.

Epidemiology

Affects 10% of infants, more common in males and colder, drier climates. Likely involves Malassezia colonization combined with increased sebum production from maternal androgens.

Clinical Features

Typically begins on scalp but extends to forehead, eyebrows, and skin folds. Presents with greasy, adherent yellow or brownish scales on erythematous, often macerated skin. Infants are typically asymptomatic.

Diagnosis

Diagnosis is clinical. Differentiate from atopic dermatitis (which is pruritic and requires aggressive treatment) by the benign nature, lack of pruritus, and typical age of onset.

Management

Conservative treatment includes gentle cleansing with mild baby shampoos. Emollients or mineral oil soften scales. Most cases resolve with simple hygiene. Low-potency topical corticosteroids or antifungal shampoos treat persistent cases.

Parental Education

Reassure parents that cradle cap is benign, non-contagious, and not related to hygiene. The condition resolves by ages 3-4 years in nearly all cases.
meta_description: Cradle cap (infantile seborrheic dermatitis): benign scalp condition with gentle management and excellent prognosis.

title: Diaper Dermatitis: Irritant vs. Candidal
content:
Diaper Dermatitis: Classification and Management

Diaper dermatitis is the most frequent dermatological complaint in infants (7-35% of children), encompassing irritant contact dermatitis (ICD) and candidal infection. Effective management requires accurate differentiation as treatment approaches differ significantly.

Irritant Contact Dermatitis

ICD accounts for 80-90% of cases, resulting from prolonged contact with urine, feces, sweat, and friction. Fecal enzymes activated in alkaline urine break down skin barrier.

Clinical Presentation of ICD

Erythema, edema, and erosion in areas of greatest diaper contact. Gluteal cleft, genitalia, and lower abdomen affected while skin folds spared. Border usually poorly demarcated.

Candidal Diaper Dermatitis

Accounts for 10-15% of cases, develops as secondary infection complicating irritant dermatitis. Candida thrives in warm, moist, alkaline environment.

Clinical Features of Candidal Infection

Bright red erythema with sharp, defined border. Satellite pustules beyond main erythema indicate yeast spread. Vulvar or scrotal involvement may occur.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis. Satellite lesions and sharp borders suggest candidal infection. KOH preparation or fungal culture confirms if needed.

Management of Irritant Dermatitis

Prevention paramount: frequent diaper changes, prompt cleansing, thorough drying. Barrier products with zinc oxide, petrolatum, or dimethicone. Topical hydrocortisone 1% for 3-5 days if moderate inflammation.

Management of Candidal Dermatitis

Topical azoles or nystatin 2-3 times daily for 7-10 days. Concurrent oral therapy if thrush present. Barrier creams combined with antifungals.

Prognosis

Most cases respond promptly to appropriate treatment. Severe, persistent disease may indicate secondary bacterial infection requiring antibiotics.
meta_description: Diaper dermatitis overview: irritant vs. candidal infection with distinctive features and targeted treatment strategies.

title: Infantile Hemangiomas: Benign Vascular Tumors
content:
Infantile Hemangiomas: Clinical Features and Management

Infantile hemangiomas are benign vascular proliferations, the most common tumor in infants (5-10% by age 1). Characterized by rapid growth phase, plateau phase, and involution phase. Most ultimately involute without intervention.

Pathophysiology

Results from abnormal endothelial cell proliferation. Proposed mechanisms include hypoxia-induced angiogenesis and abnormal growth factor expression including VEGF.

Clinical Presentation

Most appear within 2-4 weeks of life. Early lesions may be pale or mottled, gradually becoming bright red, compressible nodules. Can be superficial (strawberry), deep (cavernous), or combined. Head and neck most common (60%).

Natural History

Rapid proliferation in first 6-12 months, plateau 1-3 years, then gradual involution beginning age 1-3. Approximately 90% show involution by age 9 years.

Indications for Treatment

Most don't require treatment. Intervention indicated for lesions threatening vital functions (periocular, airway-compromising), rapidly enlarging, ulcerated, or in cosmetically sensitive locations.

Medical Management

Propranolol is first-line: 2-3 mg/kg/day divided 2-3 times. Rapidly halts growth and induces involution. Requires close monitoring for hypoglycemia, bradycardia, bronchoconstriction.

Alternative Therapies

Topical beta-blockers (timolol), oral corticosteroids, interferon-alpha, vincristine, intralesional agents. Laser therapy and surgical excision for residual lesions or specific circumstances.

Prognosis

Approximately 90% achieve involution by age 9 with minimal scarring. Early intervention for threatening lesions yields excellent outcomes.
meta_description: Infantile hemangiomas: benign vascular tumors with natural involution and treatment options for threatening lesions.

title: Port-Wine Stains (Nevus Flammeus)
content:
Port-Wine Stains: Congenital Vascular Malformations

Port-wine stains (PWS), congenital vascular malformations present at birth in 0.3% of newborns. Represent persistent dermal venules and capillaries. Unlike hemangiomas, they don't involute spontaneously and darken and thicken with age.

Pathophysiology

Result from developmental abnormalities in vascular innervation causing progressive ectasia of dermal capillaries and venules. Lack normal nerve endings controlling vascular tone.

Clinical Presentation

Begin as flat, pink or light red macules. Over time, deepen to dark red, purple, or violaceous. Follow dermatomal patterns; ophthalmic distribution (25%) carries higher risk for ocular and CNS abnormalities.

Darkening and Progressive Changes

Progressive darkening over months to years. Affected skin develops nodularity, rugosity, and textural thickening, especially in adulthood. Angiomatous nodules may develop.

Associated Syndromes

V1 distribution associated with Sturge-Weber syndrome (ipsilateral cerebral vascular malformations, seizures, visual abnormalities). Klippel-Trenaunay syndrome: PWS with limb hypertrophy and deep venous abnormalities.

Diagnostic Imaging

V1 distribution: brain MRI for Sturge-Weber evaluation. Ophthalmologic examination for glaucoma and ocular manifestations.

Pulsed Dye Laser Treatment

PDL (585-nm or 595-nm) is gold standard. Selectively targets oxyhemoglobin. Most effective when initiated in infancy before darkening. Multiple sessions (6-12+) spaced 6-8 weeks apart required.

Prognosis

75% show significant lightening (>75% clearance); 25% show partial response. Complete clearance rare. Early treatment prevents darkening and nodular disease development.
meta_description: Port-wine stains: congenital vascular malformations with associated syndromes and pulsed dye laser treatment effectiveness.

title: Congenital Melanocytic Nevi
content:
Congenital Melanocytic Nevi: Clinical Characteristics and Management

Congenital melanocytic nevi (CMN) present at birth or within weeks in 1-3% of newborns. Vary greatly in size from small macules to giant lesions. Classification by size correlates with malignant potential.

Classification by Size

Small (<1.5 cm), medium (1.5-20 cm), and large (>20 cm). Giant CMN (>20 cm or >2% body surface area in adulthood) carry 1-10% lifetime melanoma risk.

Clinical Features

Tan, brown, or black macules, papules, or plaques. Surface smooth to verrucous/nodular. Hair growth (hypertrichosis) common. Color may darken with age and develop nodules.

Malignant Potential

Small CMN: melanoma risk similar to general population (<1%). Medium: 2-5% lifetime risk. Giant: 1-10% lifetime risk. Risk distributed throughout life, not limited to childhood.

Neurocutaneous Involvement

Giant CMN covering posterior midline >20 cm: risk of neurocutaneous melanosis (leptomeningeal and brain lesions). Affects 0.5-10% with giant CMN. Brain MRI recommended for screening.

Monitoring and Surveillance

Small nevi: routine skin surveillance. Medium: dermatology evaluation every 6-12 months. Families educated on signs of transformation: size change, color variation, surface changes, bleeding, itching.

Surgical Excision

Complete excision eliminates melanoma risk. Feasible for small nevi. Staged excision or tissue expansion for medium nevi. Excision of giant CMN typically impractical.

Prognosis

Majority remain benign. Even giant CMN have relatively low absolute melanoma risk. Vigilant long-term follow-up essential, particularly for medium and large nevi.
meta_description: Congenital melanocytic nevi: pigmented lesions at birth with melanoma risk stratified by size and surveillance strategies.

title: Mongolian Spots: Benign Congenital Lesions
content:
Mongolian Spots: Common Benign Pigmentary Lesions

Mongolian spots (slate gray nevi) are common benign pigmentary lesions present at birth. Result from dermal melanocytosis. Named for higher prevalence in Asian, Native American, and African descent populations. Completely benign and regress spontaneously.

Epidemiology

Most common in Asian infants (80-90%), followed by Hispanic (70-75%), Black (40-50%), and Caucasian (5-10%). More common in males. Often multiple lesions.

Clinical Characteristics

Poorly demarcated, blue-gray or slate-colored patches/macules. Blue color from Tyndall effect with dermal melanin location. Typically on sacrum and buttocks (95%) but can appear anywhere. No elevation or texture changes.

Natural History

Present at birth or within first weeks. Gradually fade over time: 90% show significant lightening by age 5. Virtually all have faded or resolved by age 12-13.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis. Characteristic appearance, location, and ethnic background allow confident diagnosis. Distinguish from blue nevi (smaller, distinct borders) and melanoma (rare in infants).

Safety Concerns

Risk of misidentification as bruising/abuse. Mongolian spots have characteristic features distinguishing from bruises: present at birth, distinct blue-gray color without erythema or yellow discoloration, poorly defined borders, non-evolving.

Management

Expectant observation only. No treatment indicated. Parent education about typical natural history and expected resolution by adolescence essential.

Prognosis

Virtually all lesions fade or completely resolve by adolescence. No malignant transformation.
meta_description: Mongolian spots: benign congenital blue-gray lesions on sacrum that spontaneously regress by adolescence.

title: Fifth Disease (Erythema Infectiosum)
content:
Fifth Disease: Parvovirus B19 Infection

Fifth disease is a viral exanthem caused by parvovirus B19, most common in school-aged children (5-14 years). Characterized by distinctive "slapped cheek" appearance followed by lacy, reticular trunk and extremity rash.

Epidemiology

Year-round but seasonal peaks in winter/spring. Attack rate 50-60% among susceptible contacts. Incubation 7-10 days (range 4-21). Peak infectivity before rash onset.

Pathophysiology

Viral replication in upper respiratory tract with viremia and tissue dissemination. Characteristic rash represents immune complex deposition, not direct viral invasion.

Clinical Presentation

Prodrome of low-grade fever, malaise, myalgias, upper respiratory symptoms lasting 2-3 days. Then "slapped cheek" rash—intense facial erythema demarcated at nasolabial fold, sparing mouth.

Systemic Rash

Within 1-4 days: reticular or lacy erythematous rash on trunk and extremities. Net-like pattern with areas of blanching. Predominantly extensor surfaces. Intensely pruritic, worsens with exercise, heat, or bathing.

Associated Symptoms

Minimal systemic symptoms after prodrome. Fever typically absent by rash onset. Joint and muscle pain prominent. Mild lymphadenopathy possible.

Complications in Special Populations

Hemolytic anemias risk transient aplastic crisis with severe anemia. Immunocompromised risk chronic anemia. Pregnant women: 5-10% fetal loss risk, particularly first trimester.

Management

Supportive care with antipyretics/analgesics. Children return to school after rash appears (non-contagious). Symptoms resolve within 1-2 weeks; rash may persist 2-3 weeks.

Prognosis

Excellent in immunocompetent children. Self-limited infection with lifelong immunity typically developing.
meta_description: Fifth disease (erythema infectiosum): parvovirus B19 with distinctive slapped cheek rash and lacy trunk exanthem.

title: Roseola Infantum (Exanthem Subitum)
content:
Roseola Infantum: Sudden Rash Following High Fever

Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum, sixth disease) is caused by human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) or HHV-7. Characteristically presents with high fever in infants/toddlers followed by rapid defervescence and exanthem.

Epidemiology

Typically ages 6 months to 3 years; peak 6-15 months. More common in males. Seasonal variation with increased spring/fall incidence. Approximately 95% of children infected by age 2.

Pathophysiology

Lymphotropic viruses replicate in lymphoid tissues. Viremia leads to disseminated infection. Rash appears as fever resolves, representing immune response.

Clinical Presentation

Abrupt high fever onset (39-40.5°C, reaching 40-41°C) in otherwise well-appearing infant. Minimal systemic symptoms relative to fever degree. Febrile seizures in 10-15% of patients.

Natural Course and Rash

Fever 3-5 days (range 3-7). Once fever resolves, characteristic rash rapidly develops within hours to 1-2 days. Classic "rash as fever breaks."

Rash Characteristics

Rose-pink, maculopapular, non-pruritic, blanching rash. Lesions 2-3 mm diameter. Most prominent on trunk and neck, lighter on face and proximal extremities. Non-pruritic. No enanthem.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis based on characteristic presentation: high fever in infant followed by defervescence and rose-pink rash. PCR or serology confirmation rarely necessary.

Complications

Febrile seizures primary complication (10-15%). Status epilepticus and recurrent seizures rare. Severe complications uncommon in immunocompetent children.

Management

Supportive care: antipyretics and analgesics. Fever management and seizure precautions important. Antibiotics not indicated.

Prognosis

Excellent. Self-limited, typically 3-7 days total. Lifelong immunity usually develops.
meta_description: Roseola infantum: HHV-6 infection with high fever followed by rose-pink rash as fever resolves.

title: Hand, Foot, and Mouth Disease
content:
Hand, Foot, and Mouth Disease: Enteroviral Exanthem

HFMD is an acute, self-limited viral illness caused primarily by enterovirus 71 (EV-A71) and coxsackievirus A16 (CVA16). Presents with vesicular eruptions on hands, feet, and mouth in young children (typically <5 years). Highly contagious, spreads rapidly in childcare/school settings.

Epidemiology

Worldwide distribution with summer/fall predominance in temperate climates, year-round in tropics. Peak ages 1-4 years. Transmission via fecal-oral (most common), respiratory, and contact with vesicular fluid.

Pathophysiology

Virus replicates in oropharynx and GI tract. Viremia leads to dissemination. Vesicular lesions result from viral invasion of epithelial cells with inflammatory response.

Clinical Presentation

Prodrome of fever (38-39°C), malaise, anorexia, upper respiratory symptoms lasting 1-2 days. Followed by characteristic oral ulcers and vesicular eruptions on hands, feet, extending to dorsal surfaces.

Oral Manifestations

Small red macules rapidly progressing to painful vesicles. Rupture leaving painful ulcers with erythematous halos. Common on buccal mucosa, tongue, gums, hard palate. Pain may interfere with eating and drinking.

Cutaneous Manifestations

Small (3-8 mm), fluid-filled vesicles with erythematous bases on palms and soles. May extend to dorsal surfaces. Lesions remain vesicular without crusting, distinguishing from chickenpox. Buttocks and genital areas may be affected.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis. PCR testing of nasopharyngeal or stool samples confirms. Distinguish from chickenpox (progressive lesion stages), aphthous ulcers, other viral exanthems.

Complications

EV-A71 infection risks viral meningitis, encephalitis, myocarditis, pulmonary edema. Brainstem encephalitis in young children carries mortality. Post-infectious myalgias and neuropathies reported.

Management

Supportive care with antipyretics and analgesics. Soft, cool foods better tolerated. Maintain hydration. Gentle oral hygiene. Antimicrobial mouthwash prevents secondary infection.

Prognosis

Excellent for uncomplicated cases. Lesions resolve within 7-10 days. Full recovery expected.
meta_description: Hand, foot, and mouth disease: enteroviral exanthem with oral ulcers and vesicular rash on hands and feet.

title: Scarlet Fever Rash
content:
Scarlet Fever: Group A Streptococcal Toxin-Mediated Rash

Scarlet fever is acute bacterial infection by Group A Streptococcus (GAS) producing erythrogenic toxin. Presents with pharyngitis followed by distinctive fine papillary erythematous rash with sandpaper-like texture.

Epidemiology

Most common ages 5-15 years. Year-round but increased winter/early spring incidence. Transmission via respiratory droplets or contaminated food. Reportable in many jurisdictions.

Pathophysiology

GAS produces virulence factors including erythrogenic toxins (SPEs) acting as superantigens. This massive immune activation produces characteristic rash and systemic symptoms.

Clinical Presentation

Acute pharyngitis with sore throat, fever (38.5-39.5°C), headache, malaise. Intense pharyngeal erythema with exudate and cervical lymphadenopathy. Within 12-72 hours: characteristic rash develops.

Rash Characteristics

Fine, papillary, blanching erythema with sandpaper-like texture. Lesions 1-2 mm diameter. More prominent in skin folds (Pastia lines). Circumoral pallor with flushed cheeks and bright red lips. Palms and soles spared.

Associated Features

"Strawberry tongue"—erythematous with prominent papillae. Early: white-coated with red papillae (white strawberry); later: beefy red. Petechiae in skin folds. Generalized lymphadenopathy.

Diagnosis

Positive throat culture or RADT demonstrating GAS plus characteristic rash. CDC criteria: GAS infection AND fine, papillary, blanching rash with sandpaper texture.

Complications

Suppurative: abscess, otitis media, sinusitis. Non-suppurative: acute rheumatic fever (ARF), post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN). Rapid diagnosis and antibiotics prevent ARF.

Treatment

Penicillin V 250 mg orally 2-3 times daily for 10 days (first-line). Macrolides or first-generation cephalosporins for allergic patients. Alleviates symptoms within 24 hours and prevents complications.

Prognosis

Excellent with appropriate antibiotics. Symptoms resolve within 24-48 hours. Long-term complications depend on treatment adequacy.
meta_description: Scarlet fever: Group A Streptococcal infection with sandpaper-like rash and strawberry tongue, prevented by prompt antibiotics.

title: Measles Rash (Rubeola)
content:
Measles: Highly Contagious Viral Exanthem

Measles (rubeola) is highly contagious viral infection by paramyxovirus. Presents with characteristic prodromal symptoms, Koplik spots, and maculopapular rash spreading cephalocaudally. Vaccine-preventable but remains important in undervaccinated populations.

Epidemiology

R0 of 12-18 (each infected person infects 12-18 susceptibles). Respiratory droplet transmission. Virus shed from 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset. Vaccination dramatically reduced incidence.

Pathophysiology

Replicates in respiratory epithelium and lymphoid tissues. Viremia with dissemination to skin and organs. Characteristic rash represents immune response to viral antigens.

Clinical Course: Prodromal Phase

Prodrome 3-4 days: fever (often 39-40°C), "3 Cs"—cough, coryza, conjunctivitis. Koplik spots appear (small white spots with red halos on buccal mucosa opposite molars). Pathognomonic for measles.

Rash Development

Appears 3-4 days after fever onset. Begins at hairline and behind ears, spreading cephalocaudally to face, trunk, extremities over 3 days. Maculopapular, blanching eruption that may coalesce.

Rash Characteristics

2-3 mm erythematous macules and papules. Typically non-pruritic. Blanches initially; may develop petechial component in severe cases. Enanthem common. Rash intensity correlates with illness severity.

Diagnosis

Clinical: fever with "3 Cs," Koplik spots, and characteristic rash. Laboratory: serologic testing (IgM), viral culture, RT-PCR.

Complications

Otitis media, sinusitis, pneumonia (1-6%, most common cause of death), encephalitis (1 per 1000). Immune suppression increases secondary infection risk. SSPE: rare fatal complication years after infection.

Treatment

Supportive care. Vitamin A supplementation (200,000 IU 2 days, repeated 2 weeks later) recommended. Reserve antibiotics for documented secondary infection.

Prognosis

Excellent with appropriate care. Case fatality ~2 per 1000 in developed countries. Higher in malnourished children in developing countries.
meta_description: Measles (rubeola): highly contagious viral infection with Koplik spots, characteristic rash, and serious complications.

title: Chickenpox (Varicella)
content:
Chickenpox: Varicella-Zoster Virus Infection

Chickenpox (varicella) is highly contagious viral infection by VZV with pathognomonic "dew drop on rose petal" presentation of vesicles in various evolution stages. Generally mild in healthy children but carries serious complication risks in certain populations. Routine vaccination markedly reduced incidence.

Epidemiology

R0 10-12 (highly contagious). Respiratory droplet and direct vesicular fluid transmission. Contagious 1-2 days pre-rash through 5-7 days post-rash onset (until crusted). Nearly universal in prevaccination era.

Pathophysiology

Replicates in respiratory epithelium with viremia and skin dissemination. Vesicular rash from viral replication in keratinocytes with inflammatory response. Latent virus in dorsal root ganglia, risk of herpes zoster reactivation.

Clinical Presentation

Prodrome 1-2 days: low-grade fever, malaise, headache, myalgias. Characteristic rash develops in crops, beginning face/scalp/trunk before extremities. Successive crops at 3-4 day intervals.

Rash Characteristics

"Dew drop on rose petal"—clear vesicles on erythematous base. Crops show all stages simultaneously: macules, papules, vesicles, pustules, crusted lesions. Intensely pruritic. Unlike smallpox, lesions don't synchronously progress.

Course and Resolution

Fever 3-4 days. Lesions crust within 1 week of appearance. Crusts fall off 1-2 weeks later. Scarring uncommon if uncomplicated.

Diagnosis

Clinical based on characteristic rash. Confirmatory: Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells), viral culture, PCR. IgM serology confirms acute infection.

Complications

Bacterial superinfection (GAS, Staph aureus) most common. Secondary cellulitis, abscess, rarely necrotizing fasciitis, toxic shock. Encephalitis, meningitis, pneumonia, myocarditis, hepatitis. Congenital varicella syndrome if infected first trimester.

Special Populations

More severe in adults, pregnant women, immunocompromised. Pregnant women: increased pneumonia and disseminated disease risk. Congenital varicella: rare but serious malformations.

Treatment

Supportive care. Fever management; avoid NSAIDs (associated with severe secondary infection). Topical antipruritics, colloidal oatmeal. Trim nails to minimize scratching damage. Acyclovir/valacyclovir for high-risk patients within 24 hours of rash onset.

Prognosis

Excellent for healthy children. Complete recovery expected. VZV remains latent; reactivation as herpes zoster possible later in life.
meta_description: Chickenpox (varicella): highly contagious VZV with vesicles in various stages, prevented by routine vaccination.

title: Molluscum Contagiosum in Children
content:
Molluscum Contagiosum: Poxvirus Skin Infection

Molluscum contagiosum is benign viral skin infection by poxvirus. Most common viral skin condition in children aged 1-10 years. Presents with characteristic umbilicated papules. Highly contagious but benign; significant cosmetic and social impact.

Epidemiology

Worldwide distribution with higher prevalence in tropical/subtropical climates. Highly contagious through direct contact, fomites, possibly autoinoculation. Spreads readily in childcare/schools. Increased incidence in immunocompromised.

Pathophysiology

Molluscipoxvirus replicates in keratinocyte cytoplasm. Causes keratinocyte proliferation and central invagination filled with viral-laden material (molluscum bodies). Host immune response gradually controls infection.

Clinical Presentation

Flesh-colored, dome-shaped, firm papules 2-5 mm diameter. Pathognomonic central umbilication with white, cheese-like core. May appear clustered or scattered. Common face, trunk, extremities, axillae, groin. Usually non-pruritic.

Natural History

Lesions persist weeks to months before spontaneous resolution without scarring. Total course prolonged, with new lesions appearing while old resolve. Lasts months to years in some. Giant molluscum (>5 mm) may indicate immunocompromise.

Immunocompromised Patients

HIV/AIDS (CD4 <100): extensive, numerous large lesions, higher scarring risk. Other immunocompromised states: more widespread, persistent. Improves with immune reconstitution.

Diagnosis

Clinical based on umbilicated papules. Dermoscopy shows central umbilication and keratin debris. Histopathology: Henderson-Patterson bodies (cytoplasmic inclusions with viral particles).

Differential Diagnosis

Distinguish from warts (no umbilication), varicella vesicles, comedones, milia. Umbilication highly specific for molluscum.

Treatment Options

Most cases self-limited in immunocompetent children. Many parents desire treatment for cosmetic/contagious concerns. Options: topical agents (tretinoin, imiquimod, cantharidin), mechanical removal (curettage, cryotherapy, laser), rare systemic therapy.

Topical Therapies

Tretinoin nightly; imiquimod 3-5 times weekly. Both require sustained weeks-to-months application. Cantharidin causes blister formation and mechanical removal but not FDA-approved.

Ablative Therapies

Cryotherapy effective but uncomfortable. Curettage removes lesion core under topical anesthetic. Laser therapy for extensive disease but costly, may scar.

Prognosis

Excellent. Most self-resolve within 6 months to 2 years without scarring. Recurrence possible as immunity develops slowly.
meta_description: Molluscum contagiosum: benign poxvirus with umbilicated papules, self-limited course, and multiple treatment options available.

title: Warts in Children
content:
Warts in Children: Human Papillomavirus Infection

Warts are common benign skin tumors in children caused by HPV, occurring in 10-20% of school-aged children with peak prevalence in adolescence. Present with firm, hyperkeratotic papules and characteristic surfaces. Benign but cosmetically bothersome; understanding natural history minimizes unnecessary interventions.

Epidemiology

Peak prevalence adolescence. Transmission through direct contact and fomites. More common in immunocompromised. Risk factors: skin breaks, frequent water exposure, shared facilities.

Pathophysiology

HPV infects keratinocytes through skin breaches. Induces keratinocyte proliferation and hyperkeratinization. Host immune response gradually eliminates infected cells.

Clinical Types

Common warts: firm, skin-colored to gray papules with rough keratotic surface on hands, nails, dorsal surfaces. Plantar warts: thickened plaques on soles with disrupted skin lines and black macules (thrombosed capillaries). Flat warts: smaller, flesh-colored papules on face and hands.

Natural History

Critical: approximately 50% spontaneously resolve within 1 year; 90% within 5 years in immunocompetent individuals. Excellent prognosis without treatment. Treatment side effects may exceed benefit of waiting.

Diagnosis

Clinical based on characteristic appearance. Dermoscopy shows capillary patterns and red-brown dots. Biopsy rarely needed.

Differential Diagnosis

Distinguish from corns, calluses, mollusca, lichen planus. Characteristic keratotic surface and disrupted skin lines identify plantar warts.

Treatment Modalities

Given excellent spontaneous resolution prognosis, "watchful waiting" often appropriate, especially asymptomatic warts. For symptomatic or concerning lesions: topical keratolytics, cryotherapy, imiquimod, laser, surgical removal.

Topical Therapies

Salicylic acid (17-40%) daily application; 60-70% response with persistent use over weeks. Imiquimod 2-3 times weekly; 40-50% response rate.

Cryotherapy

Liquid nitrogen freezing; multiple treatments spaced 2-4 weeks required. Success 50-80%. Uncomfortable; temporary dyspigmentation risk.

Prognosis

Excellent with expected spontaneous resolution. Recurrence common but reflects persistent HPV rather than true recurrence. Most resolve within 5 years.
meta_description: Warts in children: common benign HPV lesions with excellent spontaneous resolution and multiple treatment options.

title: Juvenile Psoriasis
content:
Juvenile Psoriasis: Chronic Inflammatory Skin Disease in Children

Juvenile psoriasis accounts for 30-40% of all psoriasis cases. Presents with characteristic erythematous, scaly plaques. Significant impact on quality of life and psychosocial well-being. Understanding unique pediatric features and treatment considerations essential for optimal management.

Epidemiology

Onset ranges infancy to late adolescence; bimodal distribution (peaks 8-11 and 15-18 years). 10-15% cases begin before age 10. Strong genetic predisposition: 70-90% family history. MZ twin concordance ~70% vs DZ ~30%.

Clinical Presentation

Most common: plaque psoriasis with well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale. Distribution differs from adults; predilection face, trunk, intertriginous areas. Younger children: intertriginous onset with centrifugal spread. Inverse psoriasis (intertriginous without scale) more common in children than adults.

Pathophysiology

Complex genetic-environmental interplay. HLA-CW6 strongly associated, particularly early-onset. Dysregulated innate and adaptive immunity; Th1/Th17-mediated inflammation. TNF-alpha, IL-23, other cytokines drive inflammation and keratinocyte proliferation.

Triggers

Infections (particularly Group A Strep) trigger/exacerbate. Viral infections (varicella, respiratory) frequently precede onset. Trauma (Koebner) and stress recognized. Medications (beta-blockers, NSAIDs, lithium) may exacerbate.

Clinical Variants

Guttate psoriasis: common in children, small droplet-like papules (2-10 mm) in rapid onset, often streptococcal-triggered. Pustular psoriasis: rare in children. Erythrodermic: severe, requiring hospitalization.

Systemic Manifestations

Psoriatic arthritis less common in children than adults. Nail involvement (pitting, onycholysis, hyperkeratosis) in 25-50%. Scalp involvement frequent with significant scaling.

Diagnosis

Clinical based on characteristic erythematous well-demarcated plaques with scale. Dermoscopy or biopsy confirms in atypical cases. Elevated streptococcal titers indicate recent infection in guttate presentations.

Psychosocial Impact

Visible skin disease significantly impacts self-esteem, social integration. Depression, anxiety, poor self-esteem risks. Bullying concerns. Mental health professional involvement and peer support beneficial.

Management: General Measures

Gentle cleansing, liberal moisturizing, irritant avoidance foundational. Trigger identification/avoidance important. Group A Strep treatment may abort guttate disease. Stress management and psychological support.

Topical Therapies

Low-to-moderate potency corticosteroids first-line for localized disease. Calcineurin inhibitors for facial and intertriginous areas without steroid side effects. Vitamin D analogs combined with corticosteroids.

Systemic Therapies

Extensive disease: narrowband UVB phototherapy (first-line), conventional systemic agents (methotrexate, acitretin), biologic therapies targeting TNF-alpha, IL-23, IL-17 increasingly used in pediatrics.

Prognosis

Early-onset may have more aggressive course, higher persistent disease risk into adulthood. Variable course with remission/exacerbation periods. Appropriate management maintains quality of life effectively.
meta_description: Juvenile psoriasis: chronic inflammatory disease in children with distinct clinical variants, triggers, and age-appropriate management.

title: Childhood Vitiligo
content:
Childhood Vitiligo: Acquired Depigmentation Disorder

Vitiligo is chronic depigmentation disorder from melanocyte destruction. 25% of cases begin in childhood with unique challenges in management and psychosocial support. Significant impact on self-esteem during critical developmental years.

Epidemiology

Affects 0.5-2% population; 25% with childhood onset. Peak onset ages 10-30 years. Equal male-female distribution. Approximately 30% report positive family history.

Pathophysiology

Multiple mechanisms: autoimmune melanocyte destruction (circulating anti-melanocyte antibodies), oxidative stress with cytotoxic metabolite accumulation, loss of growth factors, melanocyte migration/differentiation abnormalities.

Clinical Presentation

Circumscribed depigmented macules and patches. Normal skin surface without atrophy, scale, or inflammation (though inflammation may precede depigmentation). Well-demarcated, sharp borders. Distribution varies localized to generalized (>50% BSA). Common sites: hands, feet, face.

Distribution Patterns

Localized (one area), segmental (unilateral, dermatomal), generalized (bilateral, symmetric), acrofacial (face and hands). Segmental: earlier onset, faster progression, earlier stabilization. Generalized: unpredictable progression.

Associated Conditions

Thyroid disease (hypo/hyperthyroidism): 20-30% of patients. Pernicious anemia, Addison disease, lupus, type 1 diabetes associated. Screen for these conditions in pediatric vitiligo.

Psychosocial Impact

Cosmetic impact profound in children. Social stigma, bullying, reduced quality of life, depression, anxiety. Early intervention and psychological support essential.

Diagnosis

Clinical based on characteristic depigmented patches. Dermoscopy shows reduced pigmentation with hyperpigmented macules at borders. Wood's lamp enhances visualization. Histopathology: absent or near-absent melanocytes.

Differential Diagnosis

Pityriasis alba (atopic hypopigmentation with fine scale, less distinct borders). Tinea versicolor (fine scale). Post-inflammatory hypopigmentation. Other hypopigmentation causes.

Assessment

Document extent (VASI or similar), pattern, progression rate. Thyroid testing recommended. Screen for autoimmune conditions. Age-appropriate discussion of associated health risks.

Photoprotection

Essential: depigmented areas lack melanin, prone to sunburn. SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen applied liberally and frequently. Protective clothing, hats, midday sun avoidance recommended.

Topical Therapies

Mid-to-potent topical corticosteroids first-line for localized disease; twice daily application. Topical calcineurin inhibitors increasingly used, especially facial/intertriginous disease, avoiding steroid side effects. Combination therapy may improve response.

Phototherapy

Narrowband UVB phototherapy for generalized/segmental vitiligo. 2-3 times weekly with dose escalation. Response rates 50-75%. Targeted/excimer laser options. Requires multiple office visits.

Cosmetic Options

Extensive disease: depigmentation (monobenzone, laser-assisted) for uniform tone. Waterproof makeup camouflage (Dermablend, Microskin). Temporary/semi-permanent makeup options for motivated patients.

Emerging Therapies

JAK inhibitors show promise in early studies. Systemic corticosteroids for rapidly progressive disease. Systemic immunosuppressants for severe disease resistant to other therapies.

Prognosis

Chronic with variable course. One-third stable, one-third slow progression, one-third rapid. Early-onset may be more favorable than late-onset. Spontaneous repigmentation uncommon (10-20% of cases).

Psychosocial Support

Comprehensive management includes psychology referral for significant distress. Support groups and peer connections reduce isolation. Family education for appropriate support.
meta_description: Childhood vitiligo: depigmentation disorder with psychosocial impact and multiple treatment options for cosmetic and functional improvement.

title: Neonatal Acne
content:
Neonatal Acne: Acne in Newborns and Young Infants

Neonatal acne occurs in newborns to 3 months, affecting 10-20% of infants. Presents with acneiform lesions (comedones, papules, pustules) primarily facial. Self-limited, resolving 3-6 months without treatment. Distinct from infantile acne with different implications.

Pathophysiology

Results from maternal androgen exposure in utero, particularly testosterone and DHEA-S. Stimulates sebaceous gland proliferation and sebum production, leading to comedones and potential inflammation. Not Cutibacterium acnes colonization or hygiene-related.

Clinical Presentation

Lesions appear within first weeks post-birth, predominantly facial (cheeks, chin, forehead). Include comedones (blackheads and whiteheads), erythematous papules, occasionally pustules or nodules. Typically asymptomatic. Severity ranges sparse comedones to extensive acneiform lesions.

Natural History

Self-limited and self-resolving by 3-6 months of age without treatment. Resolution correlates with declining maternal androgen levels. Some lesions may persist months, but complete resolution expected by 6-12 months. Scarring rare and minimal.

Differential Diagnosis

Distinguish from infantile acne (begins 3-6 months, persists longer, may indicate endocrine abnormalities). Neonatal herpes, varicella, erythema toxicum neonatorum must be ruled out. Milaria (heat rash) lacks comedonal component. Seborrheic dermatitis lacks comedones.

Infantile Acne Distinction

Infantile acne: later onset (3-6 months+), longer persistence, may associate with elevated androgens and endocrine abnormalities (evaluation indicated). Higher scarring risk than neonatal acne. May require treatment.

Parental Education

Reassure that neonatal acne is benign and not related to hygiene. Asymptomatic and self-resolving. Avoid aggressive manipulation, frequent washing, or harsh products. Simple gentle cleansing with mild soap appropriate.

Management

Expectant observation only. No treatment indicated or necessary for mild disease. Gentle cleansing with mild soap appropriate. Avoid irritating products.

Indications for Treatment

Treatment rarely indicated given self-limited course and minimal scarring. If severe or persisting beyond 6 months (suggesting infantile acne), topical retinoids or benzoyl peroxide considered. Systemic antibiotics not indicated.

Prognosis

Excellent. Virtually all cases resolve completely by 6-12 months without treatment or scarring. Lesions do not persist into childhood/adolescence. Reassurance about benign, self-limited nature primary intervention.
meta_description: Neonatal acne: benign self-limited acneiform lesions in newborns from maternal androgen exposure, resolving by 3-6 months.

title: Milia in Newborns
content:
Milia: Benign Epidermal Inclusion Cysts in Infants

Milia are common benign tiny white papules in majority of newborns, representing epidermal inclusion cysts. Typically 1-2 mm pearly white papules from keratin retention. Completely benign, asymptomatic, spontaneously resolving without treatment.

Epidemiology

Occur approximately 50% of newborns. Equal male-female distribution. No ethnic predilection. Present at birth or appear first weeks. Most common facially, especially nose, cheeks, upper lip.

Pathophysiology

Represent microscopic epidermal inclusion cysts filled with keratin. Etiology incompletely understood but likely involves incomplete sebaceous gland duct maturation with keratin retention. Not related to hygiene, sunburn, or pathological process.

Clinical Characteristics

Tiny (1-2 mm), firm, pearly white or yellowish papules. Discrete lesions without surrounding erythema or inflammation. Asymptomatic and non-interfering with function. Variable number from few scattered to more numerous distribution.

Distribution and Variants

Most commonly facial (nose, cheeks, upper lip, chin). Can appear anywhere. Intraoral milia (Epstein pearls on hard palate or gums) particularly common. Distinguish from neonatal herpes (vesicles/ulcerations with systemic symptoms).

Histopathology

Small epidermal cysts lined by stratified squamous epithelium with granular layer containing keratin. Cyst wall lacks associated sebaceous glands, distinguishing from epidermoid cysts in older individuals. Follicular infundibulum origin characteristic.

Natural History

Transient and self-limited. Most lesions disappear within 2-4 weeks to several months of age. Spontaneous resolution occurs as keratin gradually expelled or cyst ruptures. Rarely, lesions may persist several months, but complete resolution expected without treatment.

Parental Education

Reassure parents that milia are benign, normal neonatal findings requiring no treatment. Asymptomatic and self-resolving. Attempting removal or manipulation not indicated and may introduce infection or cause scarring. Pearly appearance, though cosmetically bothersome to some parents, requires no intervention.

Management

Observation and reassurance only. No treatment indicated or necessary. Attempting extraction or manual expression not recommended as may introduce infection, cause scarring, or post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. Topical treatments not indicated.

Differential Diagnosis of Persistent Lesions

Lesions persisting beyond 6 months or with atypical features (surrounding erythema, drainage, symptoms) warrant evaluation for alternative diagnoses including ectopic sebaceous tissue, steatocystoma, dermoid cyst, other cystic lesions. Dermatologic evaluation appropriate.

Prognosis

Excellent with expected complete spontaneous resolution without scarring or sequelae. No treatment ever required. No impact on long-term skin health or appearance.
meta_description: Milia: common benign epidermal inclusion cysts in newborns presenting as tiny pearly papules that spontaneously resolve.

title: Ichthyosis in Children: Overview and Types
content:
Ichthyosis: Hereditary and Acquired Keratinization Disorders

Ichthyosis encompasses inherited and acquired keratinization disorders characterized by abnormal scale accumulation. Most common is ichthyosis vulgaris affecting ~1 in 250 individuals, presenting in early childhood. Understanding different types crucial for appropriate diagnosis and management.

Classification

Ichthyosis vulgaris (most common, autosomal dominant, filaggrin mutations). X-linked ichthyosis (males affected). Autosomal recessive ichthyosis (more severe). Epidermolytic ichthyosis. Various syndromic forms. Acquired ichthyosis secondary to malignancy, thyroid disease, medications.

Ichthyosis Vulgaris

Most common, autosomal dominant inheritance. Filaggrin gene mutations impair epidermal barrier. Typically ages 1-4 years with increased scale on extensor surfaces. Small, quadrilateral polygonal scale resembling fish scales. Flexural areas spared. Associated atopy, keratosis pilaris, atopic dermatitis predisposition.

X-Linked Ichthyosis

Affects males predominantly. STS gene mutations (steroid sulfatase). Presentation typically within first month with dark brown scales. Progressive over weeks. Particularly flexural involvement. Associated cryptorchidism and increased attention deficit disorder risk. Female carriers minimal cutaneous findings.

Autosomal Recessive Congenital Ichthyosis (ARCI)

More severe phenotypes than ichthyosis vulgaris. Infants present at birth with collodion membranes (shiny, translucent covering) or severe scaling/erythema. Progressive improvement over weeks to months. Associated ectropion, eclabium, increased infection risk from compromised barrier.

Pathophysiology

All forms: disrupted epidermal barrier function and abnormal keratinocyte differentiation. Filaggrin-related forms lose important barrier protein with increased TEWL. Compensatory hyperkeratinization and scale accumulation. Other genetic mutations affecting lipid metabolism and barrier proteins contribute to other forms.

Clinical Manifestations

Hallmark: abnormal scaling varying in pattern and severity among types. Associated pruritus, erythema, hyperkeratosis, follicular hyperkeratosis (keratosis pilaris). Severe forms: systemic complications including infection and temperature dysregulation.

Diagnosis

Clinical based on characteristic scale and distribution. Age of onset, inheritance pattern, associated features differentiate types. Genetic testing confirms some forms. Histopathology rarely required. Laboratory studies exclude acquired causes.

Management: General Principles

Focus on maintaining skin barrier and managing symptoms. Frequent bathing in lukewarm water with mild cleansers. Generous emollients applied especially post-bathing. Keratolytic agents (salicylic acid, lactic acid) when appropriate. Maintain adequate humidity.

Topical Therapies

Emollients first-line, applied liberally post-bathing. Heavy occlusives (petrolatum, ointments, creams) superior to light lotions. Urea-containing products (10-40%) provide hydration and keratolytic effects. Lactic acid creams (5-12%) promote desquamation. Topical retinoids or salicylic acid with caution regarding potency and duration.

Systemic Therapies

Severe disease unresponsive to topical treatment: systemic retinoids (acitretin) considered. Particularly useful for severe forms including epidermolytic ichthyosis. Requires careful monitoring for side effects and teratogenicity. Reserved for significant disease causing functional impairment.

Management of Associated Conditions

Ichthyosis vulgaris: concurrent atopic dermatitis managed aggressively. X-linked: screen for associated conditions. Secondary infection prevention important given compromised barrier.

Genetic Counseling and Family Support

Appropriate for hereditary ichthyosis families. Understanding inheritance patterns, recurrence risk, genetic testing helps families make informed decisions. Prenatal diagnosis possible for some severe forms. Support organization connection reduces isolation.

Prognosis

Varies by type. Ichthyosis vulgaris: mild, typically stable or improves with age. X-linked: chronic but not life-threatening. ARCI: variable, may improve over time as infants transition through severe neonatal period. Quality of life significantly impacted by pruritus and cosmetic concerns, though effective management addresses most symptoms.
meta_description: Ichthyosis in children: hereditary keratinization disorders with management strategies for scaling and barrier dysfunction.

title: Ringworm in Children (Tinea Corporis)
content:
Ringworm in Children: Dermatophyte Infection

Ringworm (tinea corporis) is common fungal infection in children caused by dermatophytes, most commonly Trichophyton tonsurans in North America. Presents with characteristic annular (ring-shaped) scaly erythematous lesions. Highly contagious through direct contact, fomites, and potentially animal contact.

Epidemiology

Common in children aged 3-14 years. Higher incidence in warm, humid climates. Transmission through direct contact with infected skin, fomites (towels, bedding, clothing), animals (especially cats and dogs). Increased risk in atopic dermatitis patients and immunocompromised.

Pathophysiology

Dermatophytes invade stratum corneum, utilizing keratin as nutrient source. Trigger inflammatory response from fungal antigens. Trichophyton tonsurans most common in North America; Microsporum canis from animal contact.

Clinical Presentation

Characteristic annular scaly erythematous patches with central clearing, creating "ring-shaped" lesions. Well-demarcated borders, often advancing centrifugally. Most common trunk, extremities, face. Usually mildly pruritic. Lesions vary 0.5-5 cm diameter. May be solitary or multiple.

Diagnosis

Clinical appearance highly suggestive. Confirm with KOH preparation of scale showing fungal elements or fungal culture and identification. Dermoscopy may aid diagnosis showing scaly appearance and follicular involvement. Wood's lamp not helpful (most tinea corporis non-fluorescent).

Differential Diagnosis

Distinguish from nummular eczema (more pruritic, less defined borders, history of atopy), psoriasis (different distribution, thicker scale), pityriasis rosea (larger herald patch, widespread distribution), or other scaling lesions.

Treatment

Topical antifungals first-line for limited disease: azoles (clotrimazole, miconazole), allylamines (terbinafine), or polyenes. Apply twice daily for 2-4 weeks. Continue for 1-2 weeks after clinical clearance. Systemic antifungals (griseofulvin, terbinafine) for extensive disease or treatment failure.

Infection Control

Avoid direct contact with infected skin. Do not share towels, clothing, or personal items. Wash infected areas regularly. Remove contaminated clothing from other family members' contact. Screen family and pets for infection.

Prognosis

Excellent with appropriate treatment. Most lesions clear within 3-4 weeks with topical antifungals. Complete resolution expected. Recurrence possible from reexposure to source.
meta_description: Ringworm (tinea corporis): common fungal infection in children with characteristic annular scaly lesions treated with topical antifungals.

title: Head Lice (Pediculosis Capitis) in Children
content:
Head Lice: Pediculosis Capitis in Children

Head lice (Pediculus humanus capitis) are parasitic insects infesting scalp hair in children, particularly ages 3-12 years. Highly transmissible through direct head-to-head contact and fomite transmission. Causes intense pruritus and social embarrassment. Effective treatment available with proper application and nit removal.

Epidemiology

Most common parasitic infestation in school-aged children. Peak ages 3-12 years. More common in girls. Transmission via direct head-to-head contact (most common) and potentially through shared combs, pillows, hats, headphones. Does not indicate poor hygiene. Affects all socioeconomic levels.

Life Cycle

Adult female lice lay eggs (nits) cemented to hair shaft. Nits hatch in 7-10 days. Nymphs mature to adults in 7-10 days. Adults live 3-4 weeks on host. Can survive only 24-48 hours off host. Entire life cycle 3-4 weeks.

Clinical Presentation

Pruritus (particularly occiput and nape of neck) most common symptom. Visible lice crawling on scalp or in hair. Nits visible attached to hair shafts. Secondary bacterial infection from scratching may develop. Occipital/posterior cervical lymphadenopathy common.

Nit Identification

Nits are oval, firmly attached to hair shaft, tan to whitish color. Distinguished from dandruff (flakes off easily) and hair casts (move freely on hair). Most abundant on hair close to scalp, particularly occiput.

Diagnosis

Clinical: visual identification of lice and/or nits. Use magnification to examine hair shafts. Distinguish from other causes of pruritus and scalp findings.

Treatment: Pediculicides

First-line: over-the-counter pyrethrins or permethrin 1%. Apply to scalp and hair, leave 10 minutes, rinse thoroughly. Repeat in 7-10 days to kill newly hatched nymphs. Malathion, benzyl alcohol, or spinosad for resistant cases. Ivermectin (systemic) for severe infestations.

Nit Removal

Essential for treatment success. After pediculicide treatment, comb through hair with fine-toothed nit comb to mechanically remove nits. Repeat daily for 7-10 days. Use vinegar rinse before combing to help loosen nits.

Environmentalcontrol

Wash clothing, bedding, pillows in hot water. Seal non-washable items in plastic bags for 2+ weeks (lice die without host). Soak combs and brushes in hot water or pediculicide solution for 5-10 minutes. Vacuum furniture.

School Policies

Many schools allow return once first treatment completed and child is nit-free. Check individual school policies. Exclusion policies have been questioned as unnecessary if treated.

Prognosis

Excellent with appropriate treatment and nit removal. Recurrence common from reinfection. Prevention through education, checking for signs regularly, treating promptly important.
meta_description: Head lice (pediculosis capitis): common parasitic infestation in children transmitted by direct contact, treatable with pediculicides and nit removal.

title: Scabies in Children
content:
Scabies in Children: Mite Infestation

Scabies is parasitic skin infestation by Sarcoptes scabiei mite, highly contagious through direct contact. Presents with intense pruritus and characteristic burrow lesions. Affects all ages but features may differ in children. Requires prompt diagnosis and treatment of infected individual and close contacts.

Epidemiology

Occurs worldwide with higher prevalence in crowded conditions, childcare, schools, institutions. Transmission through prolonged direct skin-to-skin contact (most common), occasionally through fomites. Peak incidence in children 2-6 years. Can occur at any age. More cases in winter in temperate climates.

Pathophysiology

Female mites burrow into stratum corneum, laying eggs. Burrows contain mites, feces, and eggs. Host immune response to mite antigens causes intense pruritus, particularly Th1-mediated reaction. Begins 2-6 weeks after initial infestation (longer if previously sensitized).

Clinical Presentation

Intense pruritus (often worse at night when mites more active). Characteristic burrows (linear or S-shaped, 2-3 mm) visible, most common on hands, wrists, interdigital spaces, feet, genital area. In children, may also involve face, scalp, trunk. Secondary excoriations from scratching common.

Atypical Presentations

Bullous scabies: large fluid-filled bullae from intense inflammation. Crusted scabies: widespread crusted lesions with higher mite burden. Nodular scabies: excoriated nodules on genitalia and axillae. Infants and young children: may involve face, neck, scalp (unlike adults).

Diagnosis

Clinical based on characteristic pruritus and burrows. Confirm by scrapings examined with KOH prep or microscopy showing mites, eggs, or feces. Dermoscopy may visualize mite in burrow. Diagnosis often clinical without confirmation.

Differential Diagnosis

Distinguish from atopic dermatitis (different distribution, chronicity), insect bites, contact dermatitis, urticaria, or other pruritis causes. Presence of burrows and involvement of characteristic sites helps identify scabies.

Treatment: Permethrin

First-line: permethrin 5% cream applied to entire body (excluding face, scalp in older children) for 8-14 hours, then wash off. Repeat in 7 days. Apply from neck down in children (avoid face/scalp with topical agents; may use sulfur ointment 5-10% for scalp/face).

Alternative Agents

Sulfur ointment (5-10%) safe in infants and pregnant women; apply nightly for 3 nights. Crotamiton 10%: less toxic alternative but lower efficacy. Ivermectin (systemic): 150 mcg/kg orally twice, spaced 7-14 days apart. Benzyl benzoate: effective but may cause irritation.

Management of Contacts

All household members and close contacts should be treated simultaneously to prevent reinfection. Clothing, bedding, towels washed in hot water. Items not washable sealed in bags for 72+ hours (mites cannot survive long off host).

Prognosis

Excellent with appropriate treatment. Pruritus may persist 2-4 weeks even after mites eliminated (due to residual inflammation). Recurrence common from inadequate initial treatment or reexposure.
meta_description: Scabies in children: mite infestation with intense pruritus and burrows, treated with topical permethrin and management of contacts.
